name: US Constitutional Amendments
description: All 27 amendments to the US Constitution, drilled both ways: what each amendment does, and which amendment secures each right. Ratification year, confusable warnings, and one line of context on every card.

fields.back: Freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Five freedoms in one amendment; remember RAPPS: Religion, Assembly, Press, Petition, Speech.

fields.back: The right to keep and bear arms
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Its text ties the right to "a well regulated Militia," the source of two centuries of debate.

fields.back: No quartering of soldiers in private homes without the owner's consent
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). A direct response to the British Quartering Acts; almost never litigated since.

fields.back: No unreasonable searches and seizures; warrants require probable cause
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Source of the exclusionary rule: evidence from an illegal search is generally inadmissible.

fields.back: Grand jury indictment, no double jeopardy, no forced self-incrimination, due process, and just compensation for takings
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). "Pleading the Fifth" means refusing to testify against yourself; the takings clause is the basis of eminent domain.

fields.back: Criminal trial rights: a speedy public trial by an impartial jury, notice of charges, confronting witnesses, and the assistance of counsel
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Basis of the court-appointed lawyer (Gideon v. Wainwright, 1963). The 6th covers criminal trials; the 7th covers civil ones.

fields.back: The right to a jury trial in federal civil cases
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Its $20 threshold is still in the text, unchanged since 1791.

fields.back: No excessive bail, excessive fines, or cruel and unusual punishment
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Central to modern litigation over the death penalty and prison conditions.

fields.back: Listing rights in the Constitution does not deny other rights retained by the people
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). Confusable: the 9th is about unlisted RIGHTS kept by the people; the 10th is about unlisted POWERS kept by the states.

fields.back: Powers not delegated to the federal government are reserved to the states or the people
fields.extra: Ratified 1791 (Bill of Rights). The federalism amendment, invoked in every states'-rights argument since.

fields.back: States cannot be sued in federal court by citizens of another state or of a foreign country
fields.extra: Ratified 1795, the first amendment after the Bill of Rights. Overturned Chisholm v. Georgia (1793); the foundation of state sovereign immunity.

fields.back: Electors cast separate votes for President and Vice President
fields.extra: Ratified 1804. Fixed the election of 1800, when Jefferson tied with his own running mate Aaron Burr and the House needed 36 ballots to break it.

fields.back: Abolished slavery and involuntary servitude
fields.extra: Ratified 1865. First of the three Reconstruction Amendments (13-14-15: free, citizen, vote). Its exception clause still allows forced labor as punishment for crime.

fields.back: Birthright citizenship, due process, and equal protection of the laws, binding on the states
fields.extra: Ratified 1868. The most-litigated amendment; the vehicle through which most of the Bill of Rights was applied to the states.

fields.back: The right to vote cannot be denied based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude
fields.extra: Ratified 1870, the last Reconstruction Amendment. Voting confusables: 15th = race, 19th = sex, 24th = poll tax, 26th = age 18.

fields.back: Congress can levy a federal income tax
fields.extra: Ratified 1913. Overturned Pollock v. Farmers' Loan & Trust (1895), which had struck down a federal income tax.

fields.back: Direct election of US senators by the people
fields.extra: Ratified 1913. Before this, state legislatures chose senators. 16th and 17th passed the same year: tax and Senate.

fields.back: Prohibition: banned the manufacture, sale, and transport of alcohol
fields.extra: Ratified 1919. The only amendment ever repealed, by the 21st in 1933. Pair: 18 turns Prohibition on, 21 turns it off (drinking age joke: 21 legalizes).

fields.back: The right to vote cannot be denied on account of sex
fields.extra: Ratified 1920. Women's suffrage, the culmination of the movement Susan B. Anthony led; she died 14 years too early to see it.

fields.back: Moved the presidential inauguration to January 20 and the start of Congress to January 3
fields.extra: Ratified 1933. The "Lame Duck Amendment": it shortened the four-month gap between election and taking office.

fields.back: Repealed Prohibition (the 18th Amendment)
fields.extra: Ratified 1933. The only amendment ratified by state conventions rather than state legislatures.

fields.back: No person may be elected President more than twice
fields.extra: Ratified 1951, a reaction to Franklin Roosevelt's four terms. A VP who serves more than two years of someone else's term can be elected only once.

fields.back: Washington, DC gets electoral votes in presidential elections
fields.extra: Ratified 1961. DC gets no more electors than the least populous state, which works out to three.

fields.back: Banned poll taxes in federal elections
fields.extra: Ratified 1964, at the height of the civil rights movement; poll taxes had been used to keep Black citizens from voting since Jim Crow.

fields.back: Presidential succession and disability: the Vice President takes over, and vice-presidential vacancies are filled
fields.extra: Ratified 1967, prompted by JFK's assassination. Section 4 lets the VP and Cabinet declare the President unable to serve.

fields.back: Lowered the voting age to 18
fields.extra: Ratified 1971 during the Vietnam War: "old enough to fight, old enough to vote." Fastest ratification of any amendment, about 100 days.

fields.back: Congressional pay changes take effect only after the next House election
fields.extra: Ratified 1992 but proposed in 1789: a 202-year wait, revived by a college student (Gregory Watson) whose term paper on it got a C.
